content.summary: Write your first custom GLSL pixel shader in a GLSL TOP. This tutorial walks through the GLSL TOP setup, TouchDesigner's shader conventions, uniforms, texture sampling, and building a simple animated generative pattern from scratch.

sections.title: Understanding the GLSL TOP
sections.content:
The **GLSL TOP** lets you write custom pixel (fragment) shaders that run on the GPU. Each pixel of the output texture runs your shader code independently and in parallel.

Create a GLSL TOP — it automatically creates a companion **Text DAT** containing the shader source. TouchDesigner uses its own shader framework with specific variable names and conventions that differ from raw OpenGL.

sections.title: TouchDesigner Shader Conventions
sections.content:
TD provides several built-in variables and functions in its GLSL environment:

- `vUV.st` — normalized UV coordinates (0 to 1) of the current pixel
- `uTDOutputInfo.res` — output resolution as a `vec4`
- `sTD2DInputs[0]` — sampler for the first input texture
- `texture(sampler, uv)` — standard GLSL texture lookup
- Output is written to `fragColor` (or a layout location)

The shader must define a `main()` function with an output assignment.

sections.title: Create an Animated Pattern
sections.content:
Replace the default shader with a generative pattern. We'll create animated concentric rings using `sin()` and the distance from the center. The `uTDCurrentFrame` uniform provides animation over time.

This demonstrates coordinate math, trigonometric functions, and time-based animation — the core building blocks of generative shaders.

sections.title: Add Custom Uniforms
sections.content:
You can pass values from TouchDesigner into your shader using **uniforms**. On the GLSL TOP's `Vectors 1` page, add custom uniform names and values. These become available as `uniform float` or `uniform vec4` variables in your shader.

This lets you control shader parameters from CHOPs, Python, or the parameter UI without editing shader code.
sections.code:
# Configure a custom uniform on the GLSL TOP
glsl = op('/project1/glsl1')
# Set uniform name and value on Vectors page
glsl.par.uniname0 = 'uTime'
glsl.par.value0x = absTime.seconds  # This could be an expression

sections.title: Use Input Textures
sections.content:
Connect a texture (any TOP) to the GLSL TOP's first input. Access it in the shader with `texture(sTD2DInputs[0], vUV.st)`. You can connect up to 16 inputs.

Try connecting a **Noise TOP** and blending it with your procedural pattern. Combining procedural math with sampled textures is a powerful technique for creating complex visual effects.
